did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed an intelligent system for the diagnosis of malignant melanoma using computational intelligence methods. Specifically, it utilized a feedforward artificial neural network (ANN) with 3 layers and sigmoidal activation functions to classify skin lesions as malignant or benign based on extracted dermoscopic features (asymmetry, border, color). The system was trained on 80 cases and tested on 126 images, achieving 98% sensitivity and 73.07% specificity.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents the development and validation of an original intelligent system for diagnosing malignant melanoma using computational intelligence methods (feedforward artificial neural network). It describes specific methodology including feature extraction (asymmetry, border, color), preprocessing, and segmentation, and reports original experimental results (98% sensitivity, 73.07% specificity) on a dataset of 206 skin moles (80 training, 126 testing). It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: Intelligent system supporting diagnosis of malignant melanoma
journal: Advances in Intelligent Systems and Computing
brief_summary: This study developed an intelligent decision support system for the diagnosis of malignant melanoma using computational intelligence methods. The system extracts dermoscopic features based on an extended ABCD rule, specifically analyzing asymmetry, border, and color variations. A feedforward artificial neural network with three layers and sigmoidal activation functions was employed to classify skin lesions as malignant or benign. The model was trained on 80 skin moles and tested on 126 images, including both dermoscopic and non-dermoscopic types. The system achieved a sensitivity of 98% and a specificity of 73.07%, with an overall accuracy of 92.3%. The authors conclude that the system shows promising results for supporting primary care physicians and enabling self-examination.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis. The system was trained on 64 malignant cases and tested on 100 malignant cases (out of 126 total test images). The output vector Y1=0, Y2=1 indicates detection of malignant lesions.

The comparator class for binary classification. The system was trained on 16 benign cases and tested on 26 benign cases. The output vector Y1=1, Y2=0 indicates benign lesions. The paper notes that benign lesions are often solid and brown/dark brown without blue, white, or black colors.
A category for lesions that do not fall into the strict malignant or benign output ranges. If accepted as a correct result, sensitivity increases to 99%.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant melanoma or benign mole categories, with a third 'suspicious' category for ambiguous cases.
all_task_types.details: The system uses a feedforward artificial neural network to classify dermoscopic features (asymmetry, border, color) extracted from skin lesion images. The output is a vector (Y1, Y2) where Y1=0, Y2=1 indicates malignant and Y1=1, Y2=0 indicates benign. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories based on predefined output ranges for malignant, benign, and suspicious lesions.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic/Non-Dermoscopic Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of skin moles collected for the study, comprising both dermoscopic and non-dermoscopic images. The specific source or hospital is not named in the text.

use.training: Used to train the feedforward artificial neural network (ANN) with 3 layers and sigmoidal activation functions.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the system's performance, achieving 98% sensitivity and 73.07% specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics: sensitivity (98%), specificity (73.07%), and accuracy (92.3%).

Custom Dermoscopic/Non-Dermoscopic Skin.notes: The system was trained on 80 cases (16 benign, 64 malignant) and tested on 126 images (100 malignant, 26 benign). The experiment resulted in 98 correctly classified melanomas and 19 benign moles. If the 'suspicious' category is accepted as a correct result, sensitivity increases to 99%.

architecture_used.input_size: 6 inputs (coefficients A, B, C1, C2, C3, C4 extracted from preprocessing phase)

output_ranges.suspicious: Values not belonging to above ranges

classification_or_output_head.training_targets: Y1=0, Y2=1 for malignant; Y1=1, Y2=0 for benign

preprocessing_techniques: Color reduction (reducing the number of colors in the picture)
Conversion to black and white format
Masking to remove unwanted elements such as skin and hairs
conclusions: The proposed intelligent system, based on an extended ABCD rule and a feedforward artificial neural network, achieved high diagnostic performance with 98% sensitivity, 73.07% specificity, and 92.3% accuracy on a test set of 126 images.
The system can serve as a decision support tool for primary care physicians and as a self-examination aid for skin lesions using a dermatoscope.

The system's accuracy is limited by the exclusion of differential structures (pattern analysis) due to a lack of high-resolution dermatoscopic images in the dataset; incorporating these features would likely improve precision.
Increasing the number of training and testing images would benefit the system's sensitivity.
System accuracy could be further improved by integrating patient-specific clinical data such as age, weight, sex, skin type, and the anatomical location of the lesion.
important_remarks: The study excludes the 'Differential Structures' (pattern analysis) component of the ABCD rule due to a lack of high-resolution dermatoscopic images in the dataset, which limits the system's precision and diagnostic completeness.
The dataset is small (206 total images: 80 training, 126 testing) and lacks external validation, raising concerns about generalizability and potential overfitting.
The class distribution in the test set is highly imbalanced (100 malignant vs. 26 benign), which may skew specificity estimates and clinical utility assessments.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive metrics only.
The system relies on a custom, private dataset with unspecified source or hospital, preventing independent replication or comparison with standard public benchmarks (e.g., ISIC).
Preprocessing steps (e.g., symmetric blurring, fixed-parameter thresholding) may introduce bias or reduce robustness if applied to diverse clinical image qualities.
The authors acknowledge that incorporating patient-specific clinical data (age, sex, skin type, anatomical location) could improve accuracy, indicating current model limitations.
